text.title: Experimentation Platform and Best Practices
text.summary: Maturity model, modern stack components, statistical rigor requirements, and scale benchmarks for enterprise-grade A/B testing.

section.title: Maturity Model
section.p: Level 1 (Ad-hoc): individual experiments, no standardization. Level 2 (Structured): defined process, shared tools. Level 3 (Scaled): 100+ experiments/year, statistical rigor. Level 4 (Culture): every decision backed by an experiment. Scale benchmarks: Microsoft ~100k/yr, GoDaddy 1,700+, enterprise average 500-1,000/yr.

rule.statement: Pre-register the primary metric, MDE, and decision rule before traffic flips. Agents must reject any experiment doc missing these fields.
rule.rationale: Post-hoc metric selection is the primary driver of false positives. Pre-registration reduces the false-positive rate from 30%+ (peeking) to the intended 5%.

rule.statement: Never declare a winner without: lift, two-sided CI, p-value (or posterior), pre-registered primary metric, and a passed SRM check.
rule.rationale: Each missing element introduces a separate failure mode. An LLM that paraphrases vendor statistics rather than copying them verbatim will misread CIs as posterior probabilities.

rule.statement: Implement SRM (Sample Ratio Mismatch) as a hard gate on every experiment. Chi-square check (p < 0.001) on assignment counts must pass before any readout.
rule.rationale: ~6% of experiments have SRM silently. An experiment with SRM produces bogus winner declarations that persist in institutional memory even after the bug is found.

rule.statement: Apply multiple-comparison correction (Bonferroni or Benjamini-Hochberg) whenever more than one primary metric is analyzed. Document the correction in the decision record.
rule.rationale: 10 metrics at alpha=0.05 produces ~1 false win by chance per experiment. At scale (500/yr), this compounds to hundreds of false ships per year without correction.

rule.statement: Use CUPED for variance reduction on high-variance metrics. This cuts required sample size 30–50%. Confirm your platform supports it before planning runtime.
rule.rationale: Without variance reduction, experiments take longer, fewer run per quarter, and the learning rate is throttled. GrowthBook, Statsig, and Eppo all support CUPED natively.

rule.statement: Set a minimum runtime of 14 days for engagement metrics and at least one full business cycle for revenue metrics. Block winner calls before the minimum.
rule.rationale: Novelty and primacy effects inflate early positive signals. 7-day reads systematically over-estimate long-run impact on habit-forming features.

rule.statement: Always include latency, error rate, and a revenue or retention metric in guardrails. Engineering wins that tank infra are common silent failures.
rule.rationale: A variant that improves conversion by 2% while increasing p95 latency by 40% is not a ship. Without guardrails the team ships the conversion win and opens a follow-up incident.

rule.statement: After ship, leave a holdout (1–5%) running for 4–8 weeks to catch novelty decay. Document the result in the experiment registry.
rule.rationale: Novelty effects decay. A holdout is the only way to distinguish a real sustained lift from a spike that reverts to baseline after the novelty period.

antipattern.description: Experimentation graveyards: large experiment counts with no learning-extraction step.
antipattern.reason: 100k tests per year without a weekly learning-extraction pass produces a write-only registry. The organizational value of experimentation is in the applied learnings, not the count.

text.reference: Per-experiment spec with all required fields for pre-registration
Two-proportion z-test sample size and runtime calculator
SRM chi-square check: pass if p > 0.001
